title: How Datadog Used Claude and Cursor for Test-Driven Production Migration
gist: Datadog used Claude and Cursor to drive a test-driven migration of its Stream Router from an eventually-consistent FoundationDB key-value store to a relational PostgreSQL schema, cutting operation times from 45 minutes to about a second and database costs by 90% — with a comprehensive test suite as the trust ceiling for AI-generated code.

insights.claim: Datadog's Stream Router, a metrics-pipeline routing API built as an eventually consistent key-value system, hit KV transaction size limits as its routing table grew, with the most demanding operations taking up to 45 minutes due to thousands of sequential round trips.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Concrete example of KV stores failing at relational scale — a recurring driver of PostgreSQL migrations.
insights.locator: The original version of Stream Router

insights.claim: Under the KV model, Datadog's code had to reconstruct entity relationships in-process, effectively acting as a relational database inside pod processes and handling logic that foreign keys would normally enforce.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Illustrates the hidden application-layer cost of denormalized KV designs that a relational schema eliminates.
insights.locator: In the KV model, the code had to reconstruct

insights.claim: Datadog used Claude and Cursor not to generate code autonomously but to accelerate a systematic test-driven refactor: for each method they supplied the old implementation, new schema, and a failing test, and the test suite decided correctness.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: A replicable pattern for trustworthy AI migration — tests as the oracle, not the model's judgment.
insights.quote: For each method, we provided the old implementation, the new schema, and a failing test.
insights.locator: We used Claude and Cursor to accelerate

insights.claim: Three factors made the AI migration possible: strong code modularity letting Stream Router run the same API on PostgreSQL instead of FoundationDB, a comprehensive test suite as pass/fail criterion, and parallel infrastructure with a validator service comparing the two instances' routing responses.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Pre-conditions for safe AI-driven migration — teams lacking these should not attempt it.
insights.locator: According to Wakim, there were three key elements

insights.claim: The migration ran in three phases: using Claude to describe each function's intent, crafting focused prompts to fix failing tests, then a blue/green production deploy running PostgreSQL- and FoundationDB-backed routers in parallel with continuous comparison.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: A concrete phased playbook for AI-assisted backend migrations under production risk.
insights.locator: The migrations itself took place across three phases

insights.claim: Claude generated correct but suboptimal SQL queries that returned right results while issuing far more round trips than necessary, and did not independently discover niche optimizations like batching, UNNEST tricks, and common table expressions even though they exist in the literature.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: AI matches spec but not performance frontier — human optimization stays essential; tempers full-autonomy claims.
insights.quote: But they did not discover these patterns independently, even though they exist in the literature.
insights.locator: Wakim also notes a few areas

insights.claim: Once engineers wrote the optimized query patterns themselves, the AI models could replicate them across subsequent methods — but only after being shown the pattern first.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: AI amplifies human-provided patterns rather than inventing them — implies a human-in-the-loop optimization step.
insights.locator: Wakim also notes a few areas

insights.claim: The approach drove high token consumption, driven by feeding the AI full test-output dumps rather than trimmed excerpts plus the iterative loop of test output, code context, and schema.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Token cost is a real operational drag on AI migrations — trimming context is a concrete lever.
insights.locator: Another issue was high token consumption

insights.claim: After the migration, Datadog's operations dropped from 45 minutes to about a second, latencies fell from hundreds of milliseconds to a few, data storage became up to 40x smaller, and database costs were reduced by 90%.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Quantifies the upside of a relational redesign — a benchmark for similar KV-to-SQL migration business cases.

insights.claim: The key success factor in Datadog's AI migration was the strength of its test suite, which ultimately determined how much the team could trust AI-generated code.
insights.speaker: Sergio De Simone
insights.so_what: Test coverage is the gating input for AI code adoption — invest there before scaling AI-assisted refactors.
insights.quote: which ultimately determined how much they could trust AI-generated code
